title: Floor 4→6 boss encounter is 3.36x harder than preceding floor (all profiles)
description: Across all 6 skill profiles and 28,000 runs, the average turns per encounter jumps from 6.68 on floor 4 to 22.44 on floor 6 — a 3.36x ratio that exceeds the 2.0x spike threshold by 68%. Floor 6 hosts two boss-tier enemies: The Final Exam (27.5 avg turns, 77-97% win rate depending on profile) and The Burning Deadline (23.6 avg turns, 77% win rate for new_player). For weaker profiles the damage spike compounds: language_learner loses 30.7 avg HP on floor 6 encounters vs 6-10 HP on floors 1-4. This is the single largest difficulty cliff in the game.

evidence.metric: Floor 4 avg turns: 6.68 | Floor 6 avg turns: 22.44 | ratio 3.36x (threshold: 2.0x). Floor 6 death rate: 7.8% vs floor 4: 1.6%. n=7,465 floor-6 encounters across 28,000 runs

reproductionSteps: Read analytics/enemy-analysis.md, compare floor 4 vs floor 6 avg turns and death rate
suggestedFix: Reduce floor 6 boss HP or damage by 20-30%, or add a difficulty bridge encounter on floor 5 to smooth the ramp. Alternatively reduce The Burning Deadline's damage output for lower mastery levels.

title: Floor 10→12 encounter spike (2.06x turns) hits low-skill profiles disproportionately
description: Floor 12 encounters average 15.69 turns vs floor 10's 7.62 — a 2.06x jump that crosses the structural 2x threshold. Disaggregated by profile it is worse: new_player sees floor 10→12 go from 11.4→22.8 turns (2.0x), master@asc20 sees 9.4→23.2 turns (2.47x). The Curriculum on floor 12 has a 37% spike rate (>50% of HP taken in one fight) for new_player. This spike coincides with the act-2 boss tier and is structurally separate from the floor-6 spike.

evidence.metric: Floor 10 avg turns: 7.62 | Floor 12 avg turns: 15.69 | ratio 2.06x. The Curriculum (floor 12): 62% win rate, 37% damage spike rate for new_player (n=193). master@asc20 floor 10→12: 9.4→23.2 turns (2.47x)

reproductionSteps: In per-run JSON, aggregate encounters by floor and compute avg turns floor 10 vs 12
suggestedFix: Tune The Curriculum's HP or attack pattern for act 2 entry; add a floor 11 mid-tier encounter to bridge the gap.

title: Floor 18 final bosses (The Omnibus / The Final Lesson) delete 87-91% of encounters via 50%+ HP damage
description: The Omnibus and The Final Lesson on floor 18 are catastrophically damaging: 91% and 87% of encounters against these enemies respectively result in the player losing more than 50% of their starting HP. The Omnibus averages 79.5 HP taken (out of 100 starting) per encounter for new_player, with a 67% defeat rate. Average turns are 23.6-24.7 — these are slow, grinding fights that end in near-certain death for players below median skill. The floor 18 death rate across all runs is 30.2% — higher than any other floor by 6x.

evidence.metric: Omnibus: 91% spike rate, 79.5 avg HP taken, 67% defeat rate for new_player (n=323). Final Lesson: 87% spike rate, 71.4 avg HP taken, 64% defeat rate (n=278). Floor 18 death rate: 30.2% vs next worst floor 6 at 7.8%

reproductionSteps: In per-run JSON, filter encounters floor==18 and compute damageTaken/playerHpBefore ratio
suggestedFix: Reduce The Omnibus and The Final Lesson base damage by 25-30%. Consider adding a rest room before floor 18 to allow healing. The current 30.2% floor-18 death rate is the primary funnel failure point for a full run.

title: Ascension 20 is unplayable: experienced@asc20 wins 2.7%, avg death floor 3
description: The experienced profile at ascension 20 achieves a 2.7% win rate with an average of 4.0 floors reached. This is far below the too_hard threshold (target_win×0.5 = 32.5%) and the avg_floors<5 criterion escalates severity to HIGH. Of 1,000 runs, 285 (28.5%) die on floor 1 alone. This pattern persists for master@asc20 at 9.1% (still below the 32.5% threshold). Ascension 20 as implemented provides no viable path to completion — it functions as a wall, not a challenge.

evidence.metric: experienced@asc20: win=2.7%, avg_floors=4.0, floor-1 deaths=285/1000 (28.5%). master@asc20: win=9.1%, avg_floors=8.3. too_hard_threshold=32.5% (target 65% × 0.5)

reproductionSteps: Check survival rate and deathFloor distribution
suggestedFix: Audit ascension 20 modifiers — current scaling produces a run that cannot be completed by even experienced players (76% accuracy, high charge rate). Target win rate should be 10-20% for max ascension. Consider capping enemy HP scaling multiplier at asc15 and applying flat attack increases instead.

title: experienced profile at ascension 0 wins 94.5% — above too_easy ceiling
description: The experienced profile (proxy for a dedicated returning player at 76% accuracy, high charge rate) wins 94.5% of runs at ascension 0. The too_easy threshold is target_win×1.3 = 84.5%. This is 10 percentage points above the ceiling, meaning experienced players have essentially no challenge at base difficulty. The win rate variance (std_dev=0.228) is healthy but the absolute level removes tension from the majority of runs.

evidence.metric: experienced@asc0: win=94.5% (n=4000). too_easy threshold: 65%×1.3=84.5%. Variance std_dev=0.228 (healthy). Avg floors: 17.8/18

suggestedFix: Encourage experienced players to play ascension 3-5 as a 'normal challenge' baseline. Consider a soft prompt after 3 wins at asc0 suggesting higher ascension.

title: New player encounters average 13.8 turns — nearly double the 8-turn engagement ceiling
description: The new_player profile takes an average of 13.8 turns per encounter (14,693 encounters analyzed). The engagement_tedious_grind threshold is 8 turns. At 13.8 turns, fights feel like slow attrition — particularly for a new player who is still learning the system. Language_learner is at 12.9 turns. This is driven by low mastery (cards deal 3-8 damage at L0) and the inability to charge (50% accuracy), meaning every fight takes 2-3x as many attacks to kill enemies whose HP scales for mid-game mastery levels.

evidence.metric: new_player: avg 13.8 turns/enc (n=14,693). language_learner: avg 12.9 turns/enc (n=11,932). Tedious threshold: >8 turns. Master@asc20 also hits 13.0 turns/enc

suggestedFix: Raise base card damage at mastery level 0 for strike (3→5) and ensure enemy HP at floor 1 allows sub-10 turn victories without charging. New player fights should feel resolvable, not like grinding.

title: language_learner profile HP drains to 37.4 average after floor 5 — below the 40 HP healing floor
description: The language_learner profile (35% accuracy, 61% charge rate) reaches an average HP of 37.4 after encounters past floor 5 — below the balance_healing_insufficient threshold of PLAYER_HP_START×0.4 = 40. This indicates the game's healing economy (rest rooms, healing cards) is insufficient to offset the incoming damage from later floors for players with low quiz accuracy. This profile also sees 16.6% of encounters take >50% of starting HP.

evidence.metric: language_learner: avg HP after floor 5+ encounters = 37.4 (threshold: 40). Damage spike rate: 16.6% of 11,932 encounters. Win rate: 19.8%

reproductionSteps: Aggregate playerHpAfter for encounters with floor >= 5
suggestedFix: Increase healing at rest rooms for players below 50% HP. Consider giving language/vocab-focused runs a 'catch-up healing' relic that provides bonus HP recovery when accuracy is low.

title: language_learner wrong-answer rate is 64.9% — above the 60% frustration ceiling
description: The language_learner profile answers 64.9% of card plays incorrectly (265,231 wrong out of 408,824 plays). The engagement_frustrating_streaks threshold is 60% wrong/played for new-player-tier profiles. At nearly 2/3 wrong answers, the game loop for language learners is predominantly: attempt charge → get it wrong → fizzle. The 0.50x fizzle ratio is protective, but sustained 65% wrong-answer rates mean the player is more often punished than rewarded.

evidence.metric: language_learner: wrong=265,231 / played=408,824 = 64.9%. Threshold: >60%. This profile has 35% accuracy and 61% charge rate meaning most charge attempts fail

reproductionSteps: Sum totalWrong and totalCardsPlayed across all runs, compute ratio
suggestedFix: Consider reducing the charge-attempt rate for language learners who have low historical accuracy — let the bot (and real players) lean on Quick Play more. Alternatively provide a tooltip or UI hint after 5+ consecutive wrong answers suggesting Quick Play.

title: master@asc20 timeout rate reaches 5.43% — crossing the 5% structural threshold
description: The master@asc20 profile (85% accuracy, high mastery) sees 5.43% of encounters end in timeout — just above the 5% structural threshold. This is notable because it occurs in the highest-skill profile under the hardest ascension, suggesting enemy HP values at asc20 can exceed the player's damage output even with near-perfect play. Combined with the 9.1% win rate, timeout-induced stalemates are a real second failure mode at max ascension.

evidence.metric: master@asc20: 433 timeouts / 7,973 encounters = 5.43%. Threshold: >5%. master@asc15: 1.6% (within normal range). language_learner: 2.68%

suggestedFix: Cap enemy HP at asc20 to ensure even low-damage runs can technically win within the turn limit. Check if the timeout limit scales with enemy HP or stays fixed.

title: language_learner 16.6% and competent 13.5% of encounters take >50% HP in a single fight
description: Both the language_learner (16.6% of 11,932 encounters) and competent (13.5% of 13,589 encounters) profiles exceed the 10% damage spike threshold — meaning more than 1 in 8 fights drains over half of starting HP. For competent this spikes further: The Burning Deadline on floor 6 averages 30.7 HP taken vs 89 HP start (34.5% ratio per encounter, but 24% of individual competent fights go above 50% HP).

evidence.metric: language_learner: 16.6% spike rate (1,981/11,932 encounters). competent: 13.5% spike rate (1,834/13,589 encounters). Balance threshold: >10%

suggestedFix: Reduce The Burning Deadline damage scaling for players with mastery < 2. Consider a damage reduction mechanic for players at low HP entering floor 6 boss fights.

title: Ascension 15-20 win rates indicate a progression cliff where difficulty outpaces player agency
description: The engagement_low_fun rule fires when regular/gamer proxies fall below too_hard_threshold. At ascension 15, experienced@asc15 drops to 55% (within range), but master@asc15 is 31% — only marginally above the 32.5% too_hard threshold. At asc20 both profiles are catastrophically below: experienced@asc20 at 2.7% and master@asc20 at 9.1%. The progression from asc10 (72%/42%) to asc15 (55%/31%) to asc20 (2.7%/9.1%) shows a non-linear cliff rather than a smooth curve — asc20 isn't 'harder', it's a different game.

evidence.metric: experienced@asc10: 72%, asc15: 55%, asc20: 2.7%. master@asc10: 42%, asc15: 31%, asc20: 9.1%. The drop from asc15→asc20 is 52pp for experienced (vs 17pp for asc10→asc15)

reproductionSteps: Compare win rates across ascension levels
suggestedFix: The asc15→asc20 gap needs a difficulty audit. Either add ascension levels 16-19 as stepping stones, or tune asc20 so that it targets 15-25% win rate rather than 3-9%. A player should be able to see what they're working toward.

title: volatile_core relic has 6% observational win rate vs 59% baseline — power score 0.378
description: The volatile_core relic appears in 250 runs and achieves only 6.0% win rate vs 59.6% for non-holders. The survivorship-free relic-performance report confirms the signal: 7.0 floors after acquisition (vs 17.0 for top relics), power score 0.378 vs 0.680-0.700 cluster for all other relics. Per cross-checking individual profiles: volatile_core holders in experienced profile win 42.9% (n=7) vs 94.7% for non-holders; master holders win 20% (n=5) vs 71.3%. The relic is either mechanically broken (its effect may not trigger as intended) or so punishing that acquiring it is a net negative.

evidence.metric: volatile_core: 250 appearances, 6.0% win rate (-53.6% delta vs baseline). Power score 0.378 vs cluster mean ~0.67. 7.0 avg floors after acquisition. Experienced: 42.9% win with vs 94.7% without (n=7 vs 1993)

reproductionSteps: In relic-performance.md and relic-analysis.md, compare volatile_core vs baseline. Check src/data/relics/ for volatile_core definition.
suggestedFix: Audit volatile_core's implementation. The -53.6% observational delta is the largest negative relic signal in the dataset by far. Either the relic description doesn't match its effect, or it creates an unwinnable game state. Check if it permanently modifies HP/damage in a way that stacks negatively.

title: build_strength archetype at 41% win rate — 2.1x gap below build_poison (87%)
description: The 8 tested archetypes span a 2.1x win rate gap from build_strength (41%) to build_poison (87%). build_fortress at 84% and build_poison at 87% are both above the too_easy threshold for a 65%-target profile. build_strength at 41% and build_chain/build_tempo at 54% indicate these archetypes are undertuned relative to their counterparts. All archetypes share nearly identical deck compositions (42-48% attack, 46-50% shield) so the gap comes entirely from mechanic choices — strength-scaling mechanics underperform poison/fortress mechanics.

evidence.metric: build_poison: 87%, build_fortress: 84%, build_control: 73%, build_exhaust: 65%, build_chain/tempo: 54%, build_strength: 41%. HP efficiency delta: fortress=34.66x vs strength=12.62x. build_strength avg mastery 2.5 vs poison 4.0

reproductionSteps: Compare archetype-analysis.md win rates and HP efficiency
suggestedFix: Buff strength-scaling mechanics (reckless, heavy_strike, bash) or adjust the bot profile's card selection logic to better support the strength archetype's win condition. Flag for balance-sim follow-up.

title: eruption card carries -14.4% win-rate delta with 3,741 affected runs — potential archetype trap
description: The eruption card appears in 3,741 runs (via card-analysis) with a correlated win rate of 45% vs the 59% baseline — a -14.4% delta. This is the largest negative card-level win signal in the dataset. With 125,486 total plays and 2.22 average play frequency per run, eruption is a common card that correlates strongly with losing. This may indicate it is a 'trap' card that players draft because it appears useful (18.4 avg dmg) but underperforms in actual play contexts. The barbed_edge PRE-EXISTING-1 bug could be inflating eruption's effective damage in playtests while the real game is lower.

evidence.metric: eruption: 125,486 plays, 3,741 runs, win%=45% vs 59% baseline, delta=-14.4%. Avg dmg/play=18.4, floorDelta=+0.2 (near neutral on floors, but win-rate-negative)

reproductionSteps: In card-analysis.md, sort by delta and check eruption row
suggestedFix: Investigate whether eruption is being drawn too frequently in the starting deck, causing players to over-invest in the mechanic. Cross-reference with barbed_edge bug resolution — after fixing PRE-EXISTING-1, eruption's real damage output may be even lower.

title: execute card actual charge rate is 9% per encounter data vs 24% in card-analysis — possible tracking discrepancy
description: The card-analysis report shows execute with a 24% charge rate. Manual extraction from per-encounter cardPlay arrays shows only 9.0% of execute plays are charged (136/1,515 plays across 4 profiles). This 2.7x discrepancy may indicate the analytics aggregation weights charge attempts differently from actual charges, or execute has a conditional charging mechanic (enemy HP threshold) that the aggregate obscures. This warrants investigation of analytics computation methodology.

evidence.metric: card-analysis.md: execute charge%=24% (2,322 plays). Per-encounter cardPlay extraction: execute charged=136/1,515 plays = 9.0%. Discrepancy factor: 2.7x

reproductionSteps: In card-analysis.md row for execute compare charge% vs manual count from encounter cardPlays
suggestedFix: Verify analytics-report.ts charge rate aggregation logic for cards with conditional charging. Execute may only charge when enemy is below HP threshold — this would suppress charge opportunities and be expected behavior, but should be documented.

title: Gym-server comboCount NaN: RL pipeline fully non-functional (obs + reward both corrupted)
description:
The field `comboCount` was removed from `TurnState` in `src/services/turnManager.ts` (replaced by `consecutiveCorrectThisEncounter` and `chainLength`), but `tests/playtest/headless/gym-server.ts` was never updated. Two code paths reference the deleted field:

1. `buildCombatObsSlice` line 381: `ts.comboCount / 10` → `undefined / 10 = NaN` → JSON null at obs[6]. NaN propagates through neural network policy → all 30 action logits = NaN → `MaskablePPO.predict()` raises ValueError immediately after the first combat step.

2. `calculateCombatReward` line 628: `0.1 * ts.comboCount` → `0.1 * undefined = NaN` → JavaScript NaN serialized as JSON null → Python `float(response['reward'])` raises TypeError.

All 6 model checkpoints are blocked. The 2 models with 80-float obs-space (pre-v2) also fail due to obs-space shape mismatch (env now emits 120 floats). The RL testing system cannot run any analysis in its current state. No RL-derived strategy conclusions are possible.

evidence.metric: 100% of episodes fail within 1-4 steps; obs[6]=null after first TurnState activation; reward=null on any correct charge action

reproductionSteps: Observe: ValueError at sb3_contrib/common/maskable/distributions.py — logits are all NaN
Observe: TypeError: float() argument must be a string or a real number, not 'NoneType'
suggestedFix: In gym-server.ts: (1) Line 381: replace ts.comboCount with (ts.consecutiveCorrectThisEncounter ?? 0). (2) Line 628: replace ts.comboCount with (ts.consecutiveCorrectThisEncounter ?? 0). (3) Lines 870, 977, 982: replace comboCount field reads on TurnState with consecutiveCorrectThisEncounter. (4) Lines 1111, 1212: remove dead writes to ts.comboCount (the field no longer exists on TurnState). (5) After fix: verify gym-server type-checks cleanly, run 50-episode smoke test to confirm. NOTE: the trained models may not have learned optimal combo-chain behavior since comboCount in the reward signal was undefined throughout training (NaN → null reward → agent received no signal for correct charges).

title: Gym-server latest/ model checkpoint is empty — rogue-brain skill broken for fresh installs
description: The symlink `tests/playtest/rl/models/latest/` points to `tests/playtest/rl/models/20260325_162724/`, which is an empty directory with no `.zip` model file. The skill documentation (`SKILL.md`) shows the analyze workflow using `--model tests/playtest/rl/models/rogue_brain_v2_masked_2M` (a named path), but if any script or agent relies on the `latest/` symlink it will fail silently. All 6 named checkpoints are ZIP files in the models/ root, not in the dated subdirectories. The dated subdirectory pattern was intended for checkpoint saving during training but no checkpoint has ever been saved there.

evidence.metric: ls tests/playtest/rl/models/latest/ returns empty; the 6 actual model ZIPs are in the models/ root, not in dated subdirs

suggestedFix: Copy or symlink the best checkpoint (rogue_brain_v3_economy_2M.zip) into the latest/ directory, OR update the latest symlink to point to the ZIPs directly. Update the skill docs to clarify the expected path.

title: RL reward signal missing comboCount bonus since TurnState field was removed — all trained models are undertrained on charge-correct behavior
description: The `calculateCombatReward` function in gym-server.ts awards `0.1 * ts.comboCount` for each correct charge. Since `comboCount` was undefined throughout all training runs (the field was absent from TurnState), JavaScript evaluated this as `0.1 * undefined = NaN`, which JSON-serializes as `null`. The Python env received `null` reward and raised TypeError on every correct charge, causing the training process to either crash or fall back to a default. This means ALL trained models (v2_500k, v2_2M, v2_masked_2M, v3_economy_2M) were trained with corrupted reward signals for the most important positive feedback loop in the game — rewarding correct charges and chain building. The models' learned strategies are therefore unreliable with respect to charge-vs-quick decisions and chain building behavior.

evidence.metric: gym-server.ts:628 ts.comboCount was undefined throughout all training; reward for correct charges was NaN (null) for all 2M-step training runs

reproductionSteps: Inspect tests/playtest/rl/analyze.py reward signal: calculateCombatReward at line 628
Confirm ts.comboCount is undefined in current TurnState (grep src/services/turnManager.ts for comboCount — returns no results)
The training logs in tests/playtest/rl/logs/ would show reward curves; if they exist, look for abnormally low reward per correct charge step
suggestedFix: After fixing Bug A (issue-001): fix the reward signal to use ts.consecutiveCorrectThisEncounter instead of ts.comboCount. Then retrain from scratch — existing checkpoints were trained with broken reward signals and may have learned suboptimal policies. For a quick smoke test, the v2_2M plain PPO checkpoint MAY be partially useful if Bug B is fixed (reward returns 0 instead of null for correct charges), but its learned behavior should be treated as untrusted.

title: Gym-server observation space has 5 stale comboCount-dependent references producing undefined/null values
description: Beyond the two critical bugs (obs[6] and reward), gym-server.ts has additional stale comboCount references that will produce undefined/null in the response JSON on every step: lines 870 (snapBefore.comboCount = ts.comboCount), 977 (comboCount: run.turnState.comboCount), 982 (info['comboCount'] = run.turnState.comboCount), 1111 and 1212 (run.turnState.comboCount = ...). These produce null fields in the info dict returned to the Python env, which may confuse downstream logic (e.g. analyze.py line 628 `if (ts.comboCount)` reward check). The info dict's `comboCount` field is read by analyze.py to track chain behavior in the 17-category report.

evidence.metric: 5 additional stale comboCount references in gym-server.ts beyond the two critical ones

reproductionSteps: Observe: 10 hits, none of which reference the actual TurnState field (it no longer exists)
suggestedFix: Do a comprehensive rename pass in gym-server.ts: replace all comboCount references on TurnState objects with consecutiveCorrectThisEncounter. Update PrevState interface if needed.

title: Foresight (0 AP cost) is strictly dominant — always played, never suboptimal
description: Across 3 independent states (03-04, 03-14, 03-19) where Foresight appeared in hand, it was identified as the correct first play every time. The 0 AP cost makes it impossible to make a wrong decision about Foresight — it is always correct to play. This is the definition of a dominant strategy card: no opportunity cost, positive return. In state-03-19 (zero damage hand), Foresight's free draw effectively replaced a dead card slot with two live draws at 0 AP cost. In state-03-14, Foresight was played optimally before a killing blow. In state-03-04, it was identified as the setup before any kill sequence. A card with 0 AP cost and a positive draw effect has no meaningful downside and should not exist as a deck staple without a trade-off.
reproductionSteps: Load any combat state where Foresight is in hand
Observe: Foresight is always played regardless of game state
No strategic tension about whether to play Foresight exists
suggestedFix: Add a meaningful cost or trade-off to Foresight: either increase AP cost to 1 (making it a true utility decision), or change the Charge bonus to reveal intent only (removing free draw), or limit to once-per-turn. Alternatively, accept the 0-AP cost but reduce draw count to 1 card at base.

title: Slow card description misleads players into wasting 2 AP against enemy heal intents
description: In states 03-07 (Citation Needed with 'Consume remains' heal) and 03-20 (The Algorithm Phase 2 with 'Self-repair' heal), the Slow card was present in hand and the enemy's intent was a heal. In both cases, analysis confirmed Slow CANNOT cancel heal intents — only 'defend or buff' intents. The card description reads 'Skip enemy's next defend or buff' which does NOT explicitly exclude heals. New and intermediate players will routinely attempt to use Slow against heal intents, wasting 2 AP (Slow base cost) plus the Charge surcharge if charged. In state-03-07 where Citation Needed healing is paired with block-steal mechanics, this wasted AP is particularly punishing. Across the two states, this is a recurring communication failure that creates a predictable 'trap' decision without providing meaningful information to course-correct.
reproductionSteps: Play a run until you face Citation Needed or any healing enemy
Have Slow in hand when enemy telegraphs 'Consume remains' or any heal intent
Play Slow expecting to cancel the heal
Observe: heal fires anyway. 2+ AP wasted.
suggestedFix: Option A (design): Extend Slow to also cancel heal intents ('Skip enemy's next defend, buff, or heal'). Option B (UX): Add explicit tooltip text stating 'This card only works against defend and buff intents. Does not cancel attacks or heals.' with an in-combat indicator when Slow is suboptimal. Option B is lower risk and higher player-clarity value.

title: Reckless attack card becomes zero-damage dead card against any block-stacking enemy
description: In state-03-09 (Tenure Guardian with 12-18 block) and state-03-16 (Proctor with 6+ block, no Piercing available), Reckless(QP) dealing 6 base damage is entirely absorbed by enemy block. The player still takes the 3 HP self-damage but deals 0 damage to the enemy. Reckless has no pierce, no block bypass, no secondary effect — it is a pure damage card with self-damage. Against the block-stacking enemy archetype (Tenure Guardian, Proctor, Headmistress, Staple Bug, Thesis Construct), Reckless is strictly worse than doing nothing (self-damage for no benefit). This creates a class of encounters where Reckless is actively harmful to play, with no visual feedback to communicate that the self-damage was wasted. The card's high floor-1 damage value makes it appealing to pick up, but it becomes dead weight against the second most common mid-game enemy archetype.
reproductionSteps: Enter combat with Reckless in hand vs Tenure Guardian or Proctor
Observe: 0 HP damage to enemy, player takes 3 self-damage
suggestedFix: Option A: Give Reckless a secondary pierce or 'deals minimum 3 damage ignoring block' at mastery level 1+. Option B: Add a visual indicator when Reckless would deal 0 HP damage (block fully absorbs it) — at least players know before committing the card. Option C: Redesign Reckless to deal half-damage to block, ensuring it always does something.

title: The Curriculum Phase 2 QP-immunity reduces player effective actions to 1 per turn
description: The Curriculum's Phase 2 (_quickPlayDamageMultiplierOverride = 0.0) makes every Quick Play attack deal 0 damage. Combined with the Charge AP surcharge (+1 AP), a player with standard 3 AP can execute at most 1 Charged attack per turn. In state-03-12, 3 of 5 hand cards (two Strikes and Heavy Strike) were completely dead as QP attacks, and the viable Charge sequence consumed all 3 AP. This reduces the player's agency to a single action per turn — a dramatic reduction from the normal 2-3 actions. While mechanically interesting as a design concept, the implementation creates a 'no good play' state not because of interesting strategic tradeoffs but because the mechanic hard-limits the player's action economy. The fight feels like waiting rather than decision-making.
reproductionSteps: Fight The Curriculum, reduce to 50% HP to trigger Phase 2
Observe: all Quick Play attack cards in hand deal 0 damage
With 3 AP and 1 AP surcharge per Charge, play at most 1 Charged attack per turn
Notice effective actions reduced from 3 to 1
suggestedFix: Option A: Reduce the Charge AP surcharge to 0 when fighting QP-immune enemies (make 'Charge mode' free to incentivize its use). Option B: Add a visual 'CHARGE REQUIRED' overlay on QP-immune enemies so players immediately understand the mechanic. Option C: Give the player +1 AP when Phase 2 activates to compensate for the AP tax. Any combination of above would improve the experience.

title: Pop Quiz (7 HP), Mold Puff (6 HP), and Group Project Phase 2 (7 HP) trivially die before reactive mechanics have impact
description: Three enemies with reactive behavioral hooks are so low-HP that they die in 1 turn of normal play before their mechanics create meaningful decisions. Pop Quiz (onPlayerChargeCorrect: stun, onPlayerNoCharge: enrage) at 7 HP dies to any two QP attacks or one Charged attack — the stun/enrage never activates in turn-1 kills. Mold Puff (6 HP) dies to one Reckless before its poison stacking creates pressure. Group Project Phase 2 (7 HP, no block) dies to any single attack, making the 'last member standing' narrative weight meaningless mechanically. In all three states (03-03, 03-04, 03-15), the strategic analysis found zero tension because the kill was trivial. These enemies have interesting designs sabotaged by insufficient HP for their mechanics to fire.
reproductionSteps: Fight Pop Quiz on floor 3 with any standard hand
Play 2× Strike(QP) or 1× Heavy Strike(QP) in turn 1
Observe: enemy dies before stun/enrage mechanics ever fire
suggestedFix: Increase HP of these enemies to extend fights long enough for their mechanics to matter. Pop Quiz: raise to 12-14 HP (currently 7). Mold Puff: raise to 10 HP (currently 6). Group Project Phase 2: minimum 12 HP (currently 7 at phase transition). These HP values push fights to 2-3 turns where the reactive mechanics have time to activate.

title: Execute is dead in most mid and early combat states (threshold never reached at draw time)
description: Execute (bonus damage when enemy HP < 30%) appeared in 5 states (03-08, 03-11, 03-13, 03-14, 03-17). In 4 of 5 states, the enemy was not near 30% HP when Execute was drawn. In state-03-13 (Final Lesson at 64% HP), state-03-11 (Final Exam at 40% HP), state-03-14 (Rabbit Hole at 71% HP), and state-03-08 (Proctor at 77% HP), Execute provided no bonus and was strictly worse than Strike QP. Execute only becomes useful late in a fight when the enemy is already nearly dead — at which point any attack card would finish them. The card creates a 'waiting for the right moment' pattern where players hold it hoping for the threshold, but by the time the threshold is reached, the enemy often dies to other cards first. Execute as a deck filler card with a conditional that rarely fires is a dead card for most of its in-hand time.
reproductionSteps: Draw it early-mid fight when enemy is at full or half HP
Observe: Execute deals 3 base damage (no bonus) vs Strike's 4 base — strictly worse
suggestedFix: Option A: Give Execute a meaningful base value even when threshold not met (e.g., base 5 damage, matching Strike, with +8 bonus below threshold). Option B: Change threshold to 50% HP (making it trigger more reliably as a mid-fight card). Current design as a pure finisher with low base value makes it a liability until the very late game of each fight.

title: Time Warp relic + Surge turn creates zero-tension auto-kill on standard enemies
description: State-03-18 demonstrates that Time Warp's 5.0× surge multiplier makes any Charged attack instantly lethal against standard enemies. Heavy Strike CC on a surge turn = floor(10×5.0)×chain = 55 damage against a 13 HP enemy. The 'tension' element of the relic (timer halved for the quiz) is irrelevant because even wrong-charge (chargeWrongValue) on surge with Time Warp deals more than enough to kill most enemies. The design intent of creating a 'special power moment' is achieved, but at the cost of strategic interest. All surge turns become the same: charge one attack, auto-kill. The Time Warp relic may be reducing the interesting AP-allocation decisions of surge turns to a single obvious action.
reproductionSteps: Observe: any single Charged attack kills all standard enemies outright
No decision needed beyond 'charge one card'
suggestedFix: Consider reducing Time Warp's surge multiplier from 5.0× to 3.5× — still dramatically powerful (3.5× vs base 1.5×) but not an auto-kill on most enemies. This preserves the 'power turn' fantasy while retaining some AP-allocation interest.

title: {N} template artifact in 118 facts — correct answers and distractors show {1988} instead of 1988
description:
A generation pipeline bug wrapped numeric answers in curly-brace template syntax (e.g., {1988}) which was stored verbatim to the facts database. Both correct_answer and distractors fields are affected. Players would see "{1988}" as an answer choice instead of "1988". Confirmed in the live facts.db via SQL scan.

Affected categories:
- General Knowledge / Pop Culture: 70 facts
- Art & Architecture / Anime & Manga: 16 facts
- Natural Sciences / AP Biology: 12 facts
- Space & Astronomy / NASA Missions: 4 facts
- History / AP World History: 4 facts
- Social Sciences / AP Psychology: 3 facts
- Natural Sciences / AP Physics 1: 3 facts
- Natural Sciences / Dinosaurs & Paleontology: 2 facts
- Natural Sciences / AP Chemistry: 2 facts
- History / US Presidents: 1 fact
- General Knowledge / Philosophy: 1 fact

Total: 118 facts

Example: Q='In what year was Akira released?' correct_answer='{1988}' distractors=['{1979}','{1982}','{1985}','{1990}']

evidence.metric: 118 facts confirmed via: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM facts WHERE distractors LIKE '%{%' → 118

reproductionSteps: Run: node -e "const db=require('better-sqlite3')('./public/facts.db',{readonly:true}); const r=db.prepare(\"SELECT COUNT(*) as n FROM facts WHERE distractors LIKE '%{%'\").get(); console.log(r.n);"
To see examples: SELECT id, quiz_question, correct_answer, distractors FROM facts WHERE distractors LIKE '%{%' LIMIT 5
suggestedFix: Write a migration script to strip curly braces from correct_answer and distractors fields where the full value matches /{\d+}/. Run: UPDATE facts SET correct_answer = REPLACE(REPLACE(correct_answer, '{', ''), '}', '') WHERE correct_answer LIKE '{%}'; and similar for distractors field (parse JSON, strip braces from all elements, re-serialize). Then re-run verify-all-decks.mjs to confirm clean.

title: 4 facts have 'undefined' string literal in distractors field
description: SQL scan confirms 4 facts with 'undefined' string literal in their distractors field. These would render as the text 'undefined' as an answer choice in the quiz UI. This is a distractor generation bug (likely a variable that failed to resolve during generation).

evidence.questionId: 4 facts — categories unknown without full scan
evidence.metric: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM facts WHERE distractors LIKE '%undefined%' → 4

reproductionSteps: Run: SELECT id, category, quiz_question, distractors FROM facts WHERE distractors LIKE '%undefined%'
suggestedFix: Identify the 4 affected facts, regenerate their distractors, or remove those facts from the active pool (set status='draft') until fixed.

title: Grammatically malformed question detected in sample: 'How many different is Azerbaijani written in'
description: During random sampling of 10 facts from the general_knowledge category, one question had malformed grammar: 'How many different is Azerbaijani written in across different countries?' The intended question is likely 'How many different scripts is Azerbaijani written in across different countries?' The verb order is incorrect. Cannot enumerate all malformed questions via DB scan alone; spotted rate of 1/10 in the sample. With 53,269 facts, this could affect hundreds of questions.

evidence.metric: 1/10 random sample showed grammatical error

reproductionSteps: Run: SELECT quiz_question FROM facts WHERE quiz_question LIKE '%Azerbaijani%'
suggestedFix: Run a grammar-check pass on all questions under 60 characters (shorter questions are higher-risk for truncation/mangling). Prioritize questions that start with 'How many' or 'What' constructions.

title: Floor 18 damage spike: 14x jump from floor 16 (avg 2 dmg) to floor 18 (avg 28 dmg) — 24.4% death rate
description:
Analysis of 28,000 headless sim runs shows a sharp, discontinuous difficulty spike at floor 18 (The Final Lesson, The Omnibus). Floor 16 avg damage taken: 2. Floor 18 avg damage taken: 28. That is a 14x increase in a single floor transition. Death rate at floor 18 is 24.4% (compared to 0% at floors 13-16).

Floor 18 enemy details:
- The Omnibus: 11,008 fights, 64.3% win rate, avg 49 dmg taken — deadliest enemy in game
- The Final Lesson: 10,896 fights, 66.1% win rate, avg 45 dmg taken

In 200 experienced-profile runs, 368/1963 floor-18 encounters (18.8%) took more than 50 damage (>50% of PLAYER_HP_START). This exceeds the balance_damage_spike threshold.

For new players and language learners, 379/1000 and 380/1000 deaths respectively occur at floor 18 — by far the most common death floor (vs floor 6 which accounts for 122 and 156 deaths respectively).

evidence.metric: Floor 18 avg dmg taken: 28 | Death rate: 24.4% | Enemies: The Omnibus (35.7% loss rate), The Final Lesson (33.9% loss rate) | Big hits (>50 dmg): 18.8% of encounters

reproductionSteps: Check data/playtests/runs/latest/analytics/enemy-analysis.md for The Omnibus and The Final Lesson stats
suggestedFix: Option A: Reduce floor 18 enemy HP by 15-20% to soften the spike while keeping them as the definitive challenge. Option B: Add a 'warning' floor at floor 17 with a mid-difficulty enemy (currently floor 17 is missing from the data — may be a rest room or segment checkpoint). Option C: Add a floor 17 combat with moderate damage (10-15 avg) to bridge the gap from floor 16's 2 avg dmg.

title: Floors 7-16 too safe: avg 1-4 dmg taken allows HP recovery, removing mid-game tension
description:
Floor-by-floor analysis shows that after a moderate floor 6 encounter (9 avg dmg, 18.3 turns), floors 7-16 become very safe (1-4 avg dmg taken, 0% death rate). This creates a bimodal difficulty curve: challenging at floors 1-6, then a long plateau from 7-16, then a wall at floor 18.

Consequence: experienced players enter floor 18 at avg 105 HP (above starting 100 HP) — they have fully healed during the mid-game. The wall at floor 18 is experienced from near-full health, making it feel unfair rather than climactic.

Sim data: experienced profile floors 7-16 show avg HP entering those floors rising from 85 to 105. Players are net-positive-HP through mid-game.

evidence.metric: Floor 7: 1 avg dmg taken, 0% death rate, 85 avg HP before. Floor 16: 2 avg dmg taken, 0% death, 103 avg HP before. 10 consecutive floors of zero tension.

reproductionSteps: Run experienced profile sim for 200 runs
Analyze floor-by-floor encounter data from JSON output
suggestedFix: Review segment 2 (floors 7-12) and segment 3 (floors 13-18) enemy damage scaling. Current ENEMY_HP_SCALING_PER_FLOOR_BY_SEGMENT has segment 2 at 0.18 and segment 3 at 0.60 — the HP increase is there but the damage output may not be scaling with it. Consider increasing enemy damage for segments 2-3 to prevent HP recovery. Alternatively, reduce rest room heal effectiveness in mid-game.

title: experienced@asc0 win rate 95% exceeds recommended ceiling — game too easy for semi-experienced players at base difficulty
description:
The 'experienced' profile (approximate: ~70% charge rate, 80% accuracy, balanced archetype) achieves a 95% win rate at Ascension 0. The floor progression shows 0% death rate through floor 16 and only 4% at floor 18. Average final HP is 91/100 for survivors.

For context: this profile represents a player who has played 5-10 hours and understands basic charge/chain mechanics but is not optimizing. A 95% win rate at base difficulty means this player feels no challenge until the final boss.

The Canary adaptive system (CANARY_RUN_STRONG_CHALLENGE_DMG_MULT = 1.20 at 85% accuracy) is supposed to provide challenge for high-accuracy players, but the 95% win rate persists even with these multipliers active.

evidence.metric: experienced@asc0: 1890/2000 runs survived (94.5%), avg 17.8 floors reached, 0% death rate floors 1-16

reproductionSteps: Run: npx tsx --tsconfig tests/playtest/headless/tsconfig.json tests/playtest/headless/run-batch.ts --profile experienced --runs 1000
suggestedFix: Consider increasing default Ascension-0 challenge for the experienced archetype, or use the Canary challenge mode more aggressively when 5+ consecutive correct answers are seen early in a run. The CANARY_CHALLENGE_ENEMY_HP_MULT_5 (1.20 at 5-correct streak) may not be triggering frequently enough. Alternatively, the goal may be intentional (new players should win; Ascension exists for challenge) — in which case this should be documented and the threshold for 'experienced-level' Asc recommendation should be lowered.

title: Quick Play vs Charge decision degenerated: 82% charge rate shows experienced players near-always charge
description:
From 200 experienced-profile sim runs: 82% of all card plays are charge plays, 18% quick plays. Avg damage per charge: 9.2. Avg damage per quick play: 3.0 — a 3.1x ratio.

The Quick vs Charge decision was designed as the core player expression mechanism ('do you know this fact?'). In practice, high-accuracy players (76-80% charge accuracy) find charging almost always dominant because:
1. Charge Correct multiplier (1.5x base) + chain multipliers (up to 3.5x) + mastery bonuses = far exceed quick play output
2. AP surcharge (CHARGE_AP_SURCHARGE = 1) means charging costs 1 extra AP — but with 3 AP, a player can charge twice and quick-play once, making the choice mathematical not meaningful
3. The only time Quick Play is correct is when the player genuinely doesn't know the answer — but the game doesn't distinguish between 'strategic quick play' and 'don't know quick play'

For a new player, this might not be an issue (forced to quick play more). For a 10+ hour veteran, the decision has collapsed.

evidence.metric: 200 experienced runs: totalQuickPlays=16614 (18%), totalChargedPlays=75757 (82%), dmg/QP=3.0, dmg/CC=9.2, ratio=3.1x

reproductionSteps: Aggregate totalQuickPlays vs totalChargedPlays across all runs
suggestedFix: Several options: (A) Make Quick Play contribute to chain multiplier building (rewarding quick-play-heavy styles). (B) Add a Quick Play momentum system: 3 consecutive quick plays in a turn grants +1 AP. (C) Increase AP surcharge for charge plays (currently 1) to 2 for high-mastery cards, forcing some quick plays. (D) Design intent review: if charge is always dominant by design (the educational goal), this is not a bug — but it should be documented as such.

title: Trivia Dungeon setup screen overwhelms first-time players with 375 DOM elements and no guidance
description:
The Trivia Dungeon entry screen (confirmed via layout dump from live session) presents: 11 domain toggle cards, 375 total DOM elements including subcategory chips for all domains simultaneously, and a 'Start Run ▶' button at coordinates (1727, 1008) — bottom-right corner, below the primary viewport for most content.

A first-time player who clicks 'Start Run' on the deck selection hub is immediately confronted with this screen. There is no 'Recommended Starter' option, no tooltip explaining what domain selection means for gameplay, and no visual hierarchy guiding them to the start button.

The domain selection screen shows 12,871 facts across 11 domains. For reference, the DECKBUILDER.md spec notes that new players benefit from focused domain selection — but nothing in the UI communicates this.

From the layout dump: the start button is visible as '.footer-start-btn.enabled' with text 'Start Run ▶' but is at (1727, 1008) — far right edge of a 1920-wide screen, requiring the player to know to scroll or look for it.

evidence.metric: Layout dump: 375 DOM elements on trivia-dungeon-screen, Start button at (1727, 1008)

reproductionSteps: Navigate to Trivia Dungeon from deck selection hub
Observe the density of domain cards, subcategory chips, and loadout cards
Note that 'Start Run' button is at far-right edge
suggestedFix: Add a 'Quick Start' button above the domain cards that starts with the default All-Domains loadout, bypassing customization. Add a short tooltip or first-run hint explaining domain selection. Move or increase prominence of the Start Run button — it should be the most visually dominant element on the screen for a first-time user.

title: Loading bar visible during combat entry creates mid-combat visual noise
description: In the combat-basic-enc1.jpg screenshot, a 'Loading...' progress bar is visible in the center of the combat scene (below the enemy sprite area, above the card hand). This occurs during the transition into the combat state. While likely brief in real gameplay, it creates a gap in the cinematic quality of combat entry — the most high-stakes moment in the game.

evidence.metric: Loading bar visible at approximately (428, 255) in the 1920x1080 combat scene

reproductionSteps: Capture screenshot immediately at 6 seconds after scenario load
Loading bar is visible during the transition state
suggestedFix: Verify this loading bar is only visible during the asset-loading phase and disappears before the player can interact. If it persists beyond 1 second of combat entry, investigate the asset preloading strategy for combat scenes. Consider masking with a fade-in animation rather than an exposed loading bar.

title: Warm container memory degradation causes crash after 5-6 sequential save/load tests
description: After 5+ sequential test runs in a single warm Docker container, the Chromium process becomes unstable and crashes on the next test request. The mysteryEvent scenario consistently triggered this when run 6th in a sequence. Container health check still reports 'ready' but page.evaluate throws 'Target crashed'. Root cause: accumulated WebGL/Phaser state in the renderer process across tests.
reproductionSteps: Start warm container: scripts/docker-visual-test.sh --warm start --agent-id test
Run 5+ sequential tests against the same container
Run 6th test — container crashes, 'Target crashed' error
suggestedFix: Restart container every 4-5 tests in sequential test runs, OR add a /reset endpoint to warm-server.mjs that navigates to the base URL to clear GPU state

title: loadActiveRun() accepts any numeric save version without migration guard
description: loadActiveRun() checks only `typeof parsed.version === 'number'` to validate the save. A save from a future schema version (e.g. v99999) passes this check and is loaded as-is. If a future version adds required fields that old deserialization code doesn't populate, runtime TypeErrors could result. Currently no migration system exists to handle version mismatches beyond the basic version field check.
reproductionSteps: Inject save with version 99999 into localStorage
Observe: returns the save object without any migration or version check failure
suggestedFix: Add a max supported version check in loadActiveRun(): if (parsed.version > CURRENT_SAVE_VERSION) return null; — also see save-load.md 'Schema Versioning' section

title: Parallel warm container tests are not safe — scenario-state collision when tests queue simultaneously
description: When two tests are sent to the same warm container near-simultaneously (within 100ms), the second test's scenario load overwrites the first test's scenario before the first test completes. This caused mystery-event to show 'restRoom' in the parallel-run test. The warm server processes one /test request at a time but the shell script doesn't wait for the previous test to finish before sending the next.
reproductionSteps: Run: scripts/docker-visual-test.sh --warm test --scenario rest-site & scripts/docker-visual-test.sh --warm test --scenario mystery-event
The second request queues behind first but result.json captures intermediate state
suggestedFix: Never run multiple tests against the same warm container in parallel. Use separate containers with unique --agent-id for parallel testing.

title: Pre-existing test failure: damagePreviewService barbed_edge synergy test fails on non-strike cards
description: src/services/damagePreviewService.test.ts 'card without strike tag gets no barbed_edge bonus' fails: expected qpValue 3, got 5. Non-strike-tagged cards (lifetap) are incorrectly receiving the barbed_edge bonus. This is a pre-existing failure present before this track ran — git status shows no source modifications.
reproductionSteps: Test 'card without strike tag gets no barbed_edge bonus' fails
suggestedFix: Fix barbed_edge relic logic to not apply flat bonus to non-strike-tagged cards. File: src/services/damagePreviewService.ts or relic implementation.

title: ap_macroeconomics: equation_symbols pool has duplicate '1' choices in balanced budget multiplier question
description: Q: 'When government increases spending and taxes by the same amount (balanced budget), what is the Balanced Budget Multiplier?' A: '1'. The equation_symbols pool (16 facts) contains 2 separate facts with correctAnswer '1'. When distractors are drawn, both appear, showing '1' as both the correct answer and a distractor. O-QZ2 violation.

evidence.questionText: When government increases spending and taxes by the same amount (balanced budget), what is the Balanced Budget Multiplier?

suggestedFix: Deduplicate equation_symbols pool: differentiate answers ('BBM = 1' vs '1 (Keynesian multiplier base)') or use bracket notation for numeric facts.

title: ap_macroeconomics: financial_asset_terms pool length disparity 3.8x (Time deposit vs Bond)
description: Q: 'What type of bank deposit requires funds to remain for a set term...' A: 'Time deposit' (12 chars). Distractors include 'Bond' (4 chars), ratio 3x. Pool has only 5 real facts + 0 synthetics. The wide length range makes 'Bond' and 'Stock' easily eliminable as length outliers. O-QZ6 violation.

evidence.questionText: What type of bank deposit requires funds to remain for a set term and penalizes early withdrawal?

reproductionSteps: Draw question about bank deposit types
suggestedFix: Add synthetic distractors matching length of 'Time deposit': 'Demand deposit', 'Savings deposit', 'Certificate of deposit', 'Treasury bill', 'Money market fund'.

title: spanish: english_meanings_adverbs pool has length disparity 4.3x (multi-word vs single-word)
description: Q: 'What does "además" mean?' A: 'also, besides' (13 chars). Distractors 'here' (4), 'now' (3), 'there' (5). Ratio 4.3x. Spanish adverb pool mixes multi-word English meanings ('also, besides', 'even though') with single-word translations ('here', 'now'). Players identify the compound meaning as the answer without vocabulary knowledge. Anti-Pattern 2 violation.
reproductionSteps: Draw questions about además, incluso, or other adverbs with compound translations
suggestedFix: Split english_meanings_adverbs: english_meanings_adverbs_short (≤6 chars: 'here', 'now', 'still', 'even') vs english_meanings_adverbs_compound (>6 chars: 'also, besides', 'only then').

title: korean: english_meanings mega-pool (1368 facts, no POS split) - POS-TELL vulnerability across all questions
description: All 1368 korean_topik1 facts share a single english_meanings pool with no part-of-speech separation. Mixed POS distractors (verbs: 'teach', 'cultivate, grow'; nouns: 'store, shop', 'meat', 'rainy season'; adjectives: 'cheap', 'fun, be pleasant') allow players to identify answers by grammatical category rather than vocabulary knowledge. Anti-Pattern 10 violation. The target_language_words pool is correctly separate, but POS splitting is missing.
suggestedFix: Split english_meanings pool by POS: english_meanings_verbs, english_meanings_nouns, english_meanings_adjectives, english_meanings_other. Pad each sub-pool to 15+. Run node scripts/fix-pool-heterogeneity.mjs.

title: korean: same 3 distractors ('store, shop', 'teach', 'meat') appearing across majority of questions
description: In 10 sampled questions, 9 of 10 showed 'store, shop', 'teach', 'meat' as the first 3 distractors. With 1368 facts in one mega-pool, the first 3 non-correct pool members always appear. Players learn these specific wrong answers across sessions, reducing learning efficacy.
suggestedFix: POS split (Issue 4) will naturally resolve this. The Korean deck likely needs to ensure the distractor selection algorithm randomizes within the pool rather than always taking the first N entries.

title: ap_chemistry: chemistry_concepts_long_u4 pool has only 9 facts (below 15 recommended)
description: Pool chemistry_concepts_long_u4 has 9 real facts and 0 synthetic distractors. At exactly 9 items, players see limited distractor variety. The pool is unit-coherent (good), but underpopulated.

evidence.questionText: In a particulate diagram showing a reaction, the circles and molecular models drawn on the right side represent...

suggestedFix: Add 6+ synthetic distractors to reach 15+: 'transition state', 'activation energy', 'reaction intermediate', 'spectator ions', 'limiting reagent', 'theoretical yield'.

title: ap_microeconomics: curve_and_graph_names_short pool has duplicate 'U-shaped' distractors
description: Q: 'In a surplus graph, producer surplus is the triangular area below the price level and above the...' A: 'Supply curve'. The pool draws 'U-shaped' twice because two separate facts in curve_and_graph_names_short have identical correctAnswer 'U-shaped' (likely one for ATC curve, one for AVC curve). O-QZ2 violation.

evidence.questionText: In a surplus graph, producer surplus is the triangular area below the price level and above the Supply curve. This area is...

suggestedFix: Differentiate answers: 'U-shaped (ATC curve)' and 'U-shaped (AVC curve)' to make each pool member unique. Or merge the two U-shaped facts into one and add a different distractor.

title: philosophy: bracket_dates pool mixes '551 BCE' text format with {1651} bracket notation - inconsistent display
description: The bracket_dates pool has 2 BCE dates ('551 BCE', 'c. 405 BCE') as plain text and 6 CE dates as bracket notation ({1651}, {1637}, etc.). When Confucius birth year question is shown, distractors from the CE dates appear as '1651', '1637' — obviously wrong CE years for an ancient figure. Format inconsistency allows elimination without knowledge.

evidence.questionText: According to traditional Chinese sources, in what year was Confucius born?

suggestedFix: Convert BCE dates to bracket notation with negative values ({-551}, {-405}) or create bracket_dates_bce pool. This ensures all pool members render in the same format (plain numbers), making CE dates plausible wrong answers for BC-era questions.

title: philosophy: concept_terms_tiny pool semantic contamination (atomism, Water, Air mixed with Epoché)
description: Q: 'What philosophical position is Vaisheshika school best known for?' A: 'atomism'. Distractors 'Water', 'Air' are pre-Socratic element concepts; 'Epoché' is phenomenological. The concept_terms_tiny pool (28 items) mixes Hindu philosophical answers with pre-Socratic Greek answers and modern phenomenological terms. Some are eliminable by tradition when questions are tradition-specific.

evidence.questionText: What philosophical position is the Vaisheshika school best known for — its belief that reality is composed of?

suggestedFix: concept_terms_tiny is likely used as a catch-all for short concept answers. Consider splitting by tradition or era when the deck's question templates can make tradition-crossing distractors eliminable.

title: ap_world_history: 10+ questions contain 'ProperNoun this' grammar scar artifacts from incomplete substitution
description: The deck has at least 10 questions where a specific noun phrase was replaced with 'this' as a placeholder but never restored. Examples: 'Song Dynasty this system', 'European Union this', 'Ho Chi this', 'Grameen this', 'Rwandan this', 'South this', 'WWI this', 'Qing this'. These render as ungrammatical questions that confuse players and compromise educational clarity. Grammar scar pipeline artifact.

evidence.questionText: Which Song Dynasty this system selected government officials based on merit rather than hereditary privilege?

reproductionSteps: Draw questions about Song Dynasty, Ho Chi Minh, European Union, Grameen Bank, Rwanda, Korean Wave, WWI Gallipoli, Qing tributary system
suggestedFix: Run verify-all-decks.mjs grammar scar check. Manual fixes needed: 'Song Dynasty this system' → 'Song Dynasty examination system'; 'Ho Chi this' → 'Ho Chi Minh'; 'Grameen this' → 'Grameen Bank'; 'Rwandan this' → 'Rwandan Genocide'; 'European Union this' → 'European Union member states'; 'South this' → 'South Korean'; 'WWI this, fought at the Dardanelles' → 'WWI campaign, fought at the Dardanelles'; 'Qing this system' → 'Qing tributary system'.

title: ap_world_history: 'Rape of this' question corrupts critical WWII massacre education
description: Q: 'What was the "Rape of this" and why is it significant?' A: 'Nanjing Massacre'. The term 'Nanjing' was replaced with 'this', making 'Rape of this' meaningless. This is the most educationally harmful scar because the question is supposed to teach students to recognize 'Rape of Nanjing' as a historical event name.

evidence.questionText: What was the 'Rape of this' and why is it significant in the AP World History exam's coverage of WWII?

suggestedFix: Fix: 'What was the Rape of Nanjing and why is it significant...?' This is a factual question requiring the historical event name to be in the question stem.

title: anime_manga: creator_names_short pool (5 facts) has duplicate 'CLAMP' distractor
description: Q: 'Which mangaka created Bleach?' A: 'Tite Kubo'. Pool creator_names_short has only 5 facts with 0 synthetics. Two separate facts both answer 'CLAMP' (likely two CLAMP-created series: Cardcaptor Sakura and another). When distractors are drawn, both CLAMP facts appear, showing 'CLAMP' twice. O-QZ1 (borderline) and O-QZ2 violations.
suggestedFix: Merge the two CLAMP facts into one (or differentiate as 'CLAMP (Cardcaptor Sakura)' vs 'CLAMP (xxxHolic)'). Add 10+ synthetic distractors to creator_names_short: 'Yoshihiro Togashi', 'Hajime Isayama', 'Naoki Urasawa', 'Rumiko Takahashi', 'Kentaro Miura'.

title: anime_manga: manga_series_titles pool (5 facts) mixes series titles with magazine names
description: The manga_series_titles pool contains: Dragon Ball, One Piece, Shojo Comic, Bessatsu Shonen Magazine, Fairy Tail. 'Shojo Comic' and 'Bessatsu Shonen Magazine' are publication titles, not manga series. When a Dragon Ball question is asked, 'Shojo Comic' appears as a distractor — obviously a magazine, not a series. Anti-Pattern 13 semantic contamination.

evidence.questionText: Which manga series by Akira Toriyama was serialized in Weekly Shonen Jump from 1984 to 1995?

suggestedFix: Remove 'Shojo Comic' and 'Bessatsu Shonen Magazine' from manga_series_titles (they belong in publisher_magazine_names). Add series titles: 'Naruto', 'Death Note', 'Fullmetal Alchemist', 'Attack on Titan', 'Hunter x Hunter', 'Yu Yu Hakusho'.

title: medical_terminology: 31 duplicate answer instances across 13 pools cause systemic O-QZ2 failures
description: Medical terminology has multiple roots meaning the same thing (ren/o and nephr/o both = Kidney; dermato and derm/o both = skin; etc.). With these facts in the same pools, any question about one root draws the other as a distractor — showing the correct answer twice. 31 duplicate instances across 13 pools including: root_meanings_urinary (Kidney x2), root_meanings_integumentary (skin x3, hair x2), root_meanings_digestive (4 duplicates), body_systems (5 duplicates), root_meanings_reproductive (ovary x2, breast x2, uterus x2).

evidence.questionText: What does the combining form ren/o mean?

reproductionSteps: Draw question about ren/o (should see 'Kidney' twice in choices)
Also: mast/o question shows 'ovary' twice; bronch/o shows 'Respiratory' twice
suggestedFix: Append the root abbreviation to each answer to make it unique within its pool: 'Kidney (ren/o)' vs 'Kidney (nephr/o)', 'skin (derm/o)' vs 'skin (dermat/o)'. This preserves educational value while ensuring pool uniqueness. Apply to all 31 duplicates.

title: world_cuisines: technique_terms_short pool is severely semantically contaminated (16 facts, 0 synthetics)
description: technique_terms_short mixes: temperatures (480°C), physiological effects (Pain receptors), ratios (14-17 times, 20-50%), chemical components (Amino acids), establishments (El Bulli, Tandoor), biology (SCOBY, Zymology), techniques (Wok hei, Spherification, Osmosis), slang (Bitter drink, Air pumping), historical values (4 denarii, 2,000). A player sees 'SCOBY', '480°C', 'Pain receptors', '14-17 times' as quiz options — temperature and body-part answers are immediately eliminable for a fermentation culture question. Anti-Pattern 13.

evidence.questionText: What is the symbiotic culture of bacteria and yeast used to ferment kombucha tea called?

suggestedFix: Split technique_terms_short into semantically coherent sub-pools: temperature_values, biological_terms, ratio_values, technique_names, establishment_names. Each sub-pool needs 5+ real facts + synthetic distractors.

title: world_cuisines: cultural_references pool mixes historical events with literary titles (Boston Tea Party vs The Odyssey)
description: Q: 'The British Tea Act of 1773 provoked which event?' A: 'Boston Tea Party'. Distractors: 'The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe', 'One Thousand and One Nights', 'The Odyssey'. Historical political events appear alongside fantasy/literary works — immediately eliminable by category. Length disparity also present (16 vs 35 chars). Anti-Pattern 13.

evidence.questionText: The British Tea Act of 1773 provoked which event that escalated into the American Revolution?
choices: The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe

suggestedFix: Separate cultural_references into: cultural_events (historical events, treaties) and cultural_works (books, films, stories). Ensure each sub-pool has semantically homogenous items.

title: ap_world_history: 'European Union this in 1999' grammar scar in eurozone question
description: Q contains 'The euro currency, adopted by European Union this in 1999' where 'this' should be 'member states'. The question is still answerable ('20 eurozone members') but the grammar break undermines educational quality and makes the question confusing.

evidence.questionText: The euro currency, adopted by European Union this in 1999 for electronic transactions and 2002 for coins and notes, is currently used by how many eurozone members?

suggestedFix: Fix: 'The euro currency, adopted by European Union member states in 1999...'

title: Chess Tactics: interactive board never renders — fenPosition data mismatch causes silent fallback to text MCQ
description:
The chess_tactics deck has quizResponseMode: 'chess_move', fenPosition, and solutionMoves on every fact. StudyQuizOverlay.svelte DOES import ChessBoard.svelte (line 7) and HAS the chess rendering branch (line 211: 'quizResponseMode === chess_move && chessContext'). The pipeline correctly forwards all chess fields through nonCombatQuizSelector → generateStudyQuestions → QuizQuestion. The board never renders because getPlayerContext() in chessGrader.ts throws when applying solutionMoves[0] to the stored fenPosition.

Root cause: The fenPosition stored in chess_tactics.json facts is the puzzle position AFTER the opponent's setup move (the position the player sees). getPlayerContext() expects the BASE FEN before the setup move and applies solutionMoves[0] to produce the puzzle FEN. Example: fact chess_tac_4172G has fenPosition='1rb3k1/pp1pB1p1/3Pp2p/q1p2n2/2P3Q1/2bB1P2/P1R4P/5KNR b - - 3 20' with solutionMoves=['e4g4','f5e3','f1e2','e3g4']. There is no piece at e4 in this FEN, so applying e4g4 throws. The catch block at StudyQuizOverlay.svelte line 77-79 silently sets chessContext=null. Board branch at line 211 is skipped. Falls back to text MCQ with no error visible to player or in console.

Contrast: chessPuzzleService.ts stores puzzle.fen as the BASE FEN correctly (before setup move). chess_tactics.json was authored with the wrong FEN convention. Also: raw puzzle ID tag '[#Goifm]' is leaked in question text — separate content fix needed.
reproductionSteps: Observe: 4 multiple-choice buttons with chess notation text (no chess board rendered)
Question shows 'White to move. [#Goifm]' — raw puzzle ID visible in brackets
Note: StudyQuizOverlay.svelte line 7 imports ChessBoard, line 211 has chess branch — the UI code IS present
rootCauseAnalysis: chess_tactics.json stores fenPosition as the post-setup-move puzzle position. chessGrader.getPlayerContext() expects the pre-setup-move base FEN and applies solutionMoves[0] to arrive at puzzle position. When solutionMoves[0]='e4g4' is applied to a FEN with no piece at e4, chess.js throws. StudyQuizOverlay.svelte line 77-79 catch block silently nullifies chessContext. Board branch condition at line 211 is never true.
suggestedFix: Fix chess_tactics.json — store the BASE FEN (one move before the puzzle position) in fenPosition so getPlayerContext() can apply the setup move correctly. This matches chessPuzzleService.ts convention. Alternatively fix the getPlayerContext call in StudyQuizOverlay to detect already-setup positions. Also strip '[#ID]' bracket tags from quizQuestion display text (separate content fix).

title: Chess Tactics: raw puzzle ID tag '[#Goifm]' leaked into question text shown to player
description: Chess fact quiz questions display 'White to move. [#Goifm]' where '[#Goifm]' is the internal puzzle reference ID used to look up the FEN position. This is an internal implementation detail that should never be visible to the player. A player who doesn't know chess notation syntax will see this as gibberish.
suggestedFix: Strip the [#ID] bracket tag from displayed question text. The quizQuestion field in chess_tactics facts should be just 'White to move.' with the puzzle ID stored separately (or omitted entirely, since the id field already identifies the fact).

title: Map Pin Drop: MapPinDrop.svelte not wired into StudyQuizOverlay — no map_pin branch in study quiz template
description:
The world_capitals deck has quizResponseMode: 'map_pin' and mapCoordinates [lat, lon] on every fact. The data pipeline correctly forwards mapCoordinates and quizResponseMode through nonCombatQuizSelector and generateStudyQuestions (confirmed by code inspection). However, StudyQuizOverlay.svelte has NO import of MapPinDrop.svelte and NO conditional branch for 'map_pin' quizResponseMode in its template. The template (lines 210-252) handles: chess_move (with chessContext), image_answers, and default text MCQ. There is no map_pin case. The restStudy flow silently falls back to standard 4-choice text answers for world_capitals facts.

This is structurally different from the chess issue: chess has all UI code present but fails on a data bug. Map pin has no UI code in the study flow at all. MapPinDrop.svelte exists and is functional — it is wired in CardExpanded.svelte and CardCombatOverlay.svelte — but never imported or used in StudyQuizOverlay.svelte.
reproductionSteps: Observe: standard 4-choice text quiz, no SVG world map, no draggable pin
Grep StudyQuizOverlay.svelte for 'MapPinDrop' or 'map_pin' — zero results (confirmed via code inspection)
suggestedFix: Import MapPinDrop into StudyQuizOverlay.svelte and add a '{:else if currentQuestion.quizResponseMode === "map_pin" && currentQuestion.mapCoordinates}' branch in the answers block (between the chess_move block and image_answers block). Wire up a handleMapPinConfirm callback similar to how CardExpanded.svelte handles it. The MapPinDrop component already implements distance-based partial credit scoring.

title: Trivia Night: TriviaRoundScreen.svelte is an orphan component — unreachable from any screen flow
description: TriviaRoundScreen.svelte exists in src/ui/components/ but is NEVER imported into CardApp.svelte or any other component. The 'trivia_night' mode is defined in multiplayerTypes.ts and referenced in MultiplayerMenu.svelte and MultiplayerLobby.svelte, but those screens require an active multiplayer lobby session with peer connections. The trivia_night game mode as a standalone single-player feature does not exist. There is no scenario preset and no screen routing for a solo trivia round. The component is dead code.
reproductionSteps: grep -rn 'TriviaRoundScreen' src/ — only self-reference in the component file
There is no scenario preset 'trivia-night' or 'trivia-round' in scenarioSimulator.ts
The trivia_night mode is locked behind multiplayer lobby (needs P2P connection)
suggestedFix: Two paths: (1) Wire TriviaRoundScreen.svelte into CardApp.svelte as a solo party-mode screen accessible from hub, add scenario preset 'trivia-night'. (2) Accept it's multiplayer-only and add a proper 'solo trivia round' mode using the trivia_night game mode type. The current state — a fully-built component with zero integration — is wasted work that could be shipped.

title: Procedural Math: accuracy counter shows 0% immediately after first incorrect answer, discouraging early learners
description: The top bar shows '1 | 0% | Stop' after one answered question. The '0%' reads as a failure state, but it's because the player answered incorrectly (we deliberately chose wrong for testing). The UX concern: the 0% accuracy metric is displayed prominently and could be discouraging for new learners who get a few wrong at first. The sad-face emoji on the Study Complete screen ('0 of 3 cards mastered up!') reinforces this.
reproductionSteps: Navigate to Study Temple → Math → Arithmetic → START PRACTICE
suggestedFix: Consider showing accuracy only after 5+ answers (same as Duolingo/Anki pattern) or replace the 0% with a more neutral label during early session. Also consider replacing the sad-face emoji on the Complete screen with a neutral or encouraging icon when accuracy is low.

title: Study Temple full FSRS session: study-quiz scenario shows empty state in fresh container — requires pre-seeded player data
description: The 'study-quiz' scenario (restStudy with no deckId) shows empty state ('Return to Hub' button, study-empty-state testid) in a fresh Docker container. This is because the FSRS queue requires pre-existing PlayerFactState records — new cards aren't served by the legacy trivia path without a bootstrapped player save with cards in the 'review' state. This makes the scenario unusable for fresh test environments. The study-deck-* scenarios work correctly (new cards are served via the curated deck Anki selector).
reproductionSteps: Observe: empty state with 'Return to Hub' button, no quiz questions
suggestedFix: The study-quiz scenario should either (a) bootstrap with some pre-seeded fact states to populate the FSRS queue, or (b) add a comment in scenarioSimulator.ts that this scenario requires prior player data. The deck-specific scenarios (study-deck-chess etc.) work correctly and should be used for fresh-container testing.

title: scenarioSimulator.ts bootstrapRun() ignores config.ascension — combat stats remain at A0
description: bootstrapRun() in src/dev/scenarioSimulator.ts calls createRunState() on line 651 WITHOUT passing ascensionLevel. When __rrScenario.spawn() is called with runOverrides: { ascensionLevel: N }, the encounter is already initialized at A0 multipliers (enemyHpMultiplier=1.0, enemyDamageMultiplier=1.0). The runOverrides patch applied afterward updates runState.ascensionLevel and runState.ascensionModifiers, which causes the HUD badge to show the correct level (e.g. 'A5'), but the TurnState ascension fields (ascensionEnemyDamageMultiplier, ascensionWrongAnswerSelfDamage, etc.) were already computed at A0 by encounterBridge.ts when startEncounterForRoom() was called. The label says A5; the enemy is actually at A0 difficulty.

evidence.bannerText: N/A - only A0 visual captured; badge would show AN for N>0 but combat is at A0 stats
evidence.expectedStat: For A10: enemyDamageMultiplier should be 1.20 in TurnState
evidence.rootCause: bootstrapRun() calls createRunState(domain, domain, { selectedArchetype, starterDeckSize, startingAp }) without passing ascensionLevel from config

reproductionSteps: Call window.__rrScenario.spawn({ screen: 'combat', enemy: 'page_flutter', runOverrides: { ascensionLevel: 10 } })
Actual: 1.00 (A0 value because encounter was started before runOverrides was applied)
suggestedFix: In bootstrapRun() at src/dev/scenarioSimulator.ts line 651, pass ascensionLevel: config.ascension ?? 0 to createRunState(). Example: createRunState(domain, domain, { selectedArchetype: 'balanced', starterDeckSize: 15, startingAp: 3, ascensionLevel: config.ascension ?? 0 }). This ensures encounterBridge.ts reads the correct modifiers when it calls getAscensionModifiers(run.ascensionLevel).
scope: Dev tool only - real gameplay (runs started via gameFlowController) is not affected. createRunState() in real runs correctly receives ascensionLevel from the player's selected ascension level.

title: A0->A5 drop is steepest in the ascension curve: 42pp drop for developing players
description: Headless sim shows: A0 developing win% = 63%, A5 developing win% = 21% — a 42 percentage point drop. This is the single largest drop in the entire A0-A20 progression for the developing profile. A5->A10 drops only 10pp. The steepness at A5 may cause new players to feel a harsh wall when first trying ascension. By comparison, experienced players drop only 14pp (93% -> 79%), suggesting the difficulty spike disproportionately affects lower-skill players. Note: this may be intentional design ('no compensating buffs A1-A7' per ascension docs), but the ratio is very steep.

evidence.bannerText: Not captured (Docker visual unavailable for A5)
evidence.expectedStat: A5 win rate for developing profile should be noticeably lower than A0 (correct design direction)

reproductionSteps: Run: npx tsx --tsconfig tests/playtest/headless/tsconfig.json tests/playtest/headless/run-batch.ts --runs 300 --profile developing --ascension 5
suggestedFix: Monitor real player data to verify whether the A5 wall causes drop-off. If player retention data shows A5 is a common stopping point, consider softening the A2 enemy damage bonus (currently +15%) or moving one challenge from A4 timer to a later level.

title: A20 experienced profile at 1% win rate is borderline below the documented 5-15% target
description: Headless sim shows A20 win rate for experienced profile = 1% (3 wins in 300 runs). Docs/ascension.ts say 'master at A20 still 82% (from April 2026-04-05 sim) — further tuning needed'. The experienced profile (76% accuracy) is not the same as the 'master' profile (100% accuracy in old tests). The quiz-pressure mechanics at A20 (wrongAnswerSelfDamage=5, forceHardQuestionFormats=true, preferCloseDistractors=true) are not captured by the headless sim since it doesn't simulate quiz difficulty variation. Real high-skill players will achieve higher win rates. This issue is LOW severity because the documented expectation was already that sim results don't reflect real quiz pressure.

evidence.bannerText: Not captured (Docker visual unavailable for A20)
evidence.expectedStat: 5-15% win rate for master/experienced players at A20 (docs target)
evidence.note: Sim cannot model quiz difficulty mechanics - real win rate will be higher

reproductionSteps: Run: npx tsx --tsconfig tests/playtest/headless/tsconfig.json tests/playtest/headless/run-batch.ts --runs 300 --profile experienced --ascension 20
suggestedFix: No immediate action. Add a 'master' profile to the headless sim (100% accuracy, optimal strategy) to get a better A20 reference. Monitor with real player data once the game ships with ascension unlocked.

title: Docker warm container crashes on __rrScenario.load() for combat scenarios — ascension visual testing blind
description: Docker warm-container visual testing is unreliable for combat scenarios. The warm server calls page.evaluate() with __rrScenario.load('combat-basic') which triggers bootstrapRun() + Phaser scene restart. In SwiftShader/Docker, this causes the Chrome process to crash (OOM/SwiftShader GPU memory limit). The 'Target crashed' error from Playwright indicates a full browser process crash, not a timeout. The warm server has no crash-recovery logic (no page.reload() or context.newPage() on crash). This means combat-state ascension testing via Docker visual is only possible for ONE test per container boot. A0 visual was captured; A5-A20 were not.

evidence.bannerText: A0 only — no ascension badge (correct for level 0)
evidence.expectedStat: Should capture HUD screenshots for all 5 levels showing correct ascension badge
evidence.actualStat: Only A0 captured; A5-A20 Docker visual testing failed
evidence.containerLogs: Test 'combat-basic' done in 8ms (FAIL) — page.evaluate: Target crashed

reproductionSteps: Observe: Test fails in <10ms with 'page.evaluate: Target crashed'
This is reproducible on every fresh container first test
suggestedFix: Add crash recovery to docker/playwright-xvfb/warm-server.mjs: on 'Target crashed' error, call page.reload() or context.newPage(), wait for __rrScenario to reinitialize (page.waitForFunction), then retry the test. Alternatively, modify visual-test-runner.mjs to not use page.evaluate() for scenario loading — instead navigate to a URL with ?devScenario=combat-basic query param if the game supports it.

title: crit_lens relic uses Math.random() — co-op peers roll independent crits for same card play
description: In relicEffectResolver.ts line 1647, the crit_lens relic's 25% crit chance uses a bare Math.random() call. In co-op mode, both clients independently compute damage for the shared enemy via delta submission. If one client crits and the other doesn't, the two deltas submitted to the host barrier will differ for the same card play. The host merges deltas additively, so the final reconciled enemy HP will be correct, BUT the crit outcome feedback shown to each player will differ — one sees a crit flash, the other doesn't. If the delta computation itself uses crit to modify the damage value that goes into the delta, the two clients will submit different damage values for the same card, leading to inflated or deflated total damage in the merged snapshot.

evidence.coopMechanism: EnemyTurnDelta.damageDealt = preTurnSnapshot.currentHP - enemy.currentHP after all card plays; if crit doubles a card's damage on one client but not the other, the deltas diverge

reproductionSteps: Two co-op peers, both holding crit_lens relic
Either peer plays a Charge card during their turn
Peer A crits (25% chance), Peer B does not
Both submit EnemyTurnDelta to the barrier
Peer A's delta shows higher damageDealt than Peer B's for the same card
Host merges: result is sum of both deltas — enemy takes non-crit damage twice instead of crit + non-crit
suggestedFix: Replace Math.random() in crit_lens with getRunRng('relicEffects').next() when isRunRngActive(). In co-op mode, the host should be the sole randomness authority for relic effects that affect shared enemy state — broadcast the crit result alongside the delta.

title: obsidian_dice relic uses Math.random() — co-op peers independently roll +50%/-25% multiplier
description: In relicEffectResolver.ts line 1712, the obsidian_dice relic applies a 50/50 multiplier roll (+50% or -25%) using Math.random(). Same issue as crit_lens: in co-op, both clients resolve the effect independently against their local enemy copy. If one rolls high and the other rolls low, the damage deltas submitted to the turn-end barrier will disagree for the same card play event.
